title: Macaulay
content:
The dreamy rhymerï¿½s measurï¿½d snore
Falls heavy on our ears no more;
And by long strides are left behind
The dear delights of woman-kind,
Who win their battles like their loves,
In satin waistcoats and kid gloves,
And have achievï¿½d the crowning work
When they have trussï¿½d and skewerï¿½d a Turk.
Another comes with stouter tread,
And stalks among the statelier dead.
He rushes on, and hails by turns
High-crested Scott, broad-breasted Burns,
And shows the British youth, who neï¿½er
Will lag behind, what Romans were,
When all the Tuscans and their Lars
Shouted, and shook the towers of Mars.
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Walter Savage Landor delivers a powerful performance in "Macaulay"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
